[[_TOC_]]

# Prerequisites

# Introduction To Power Premonition Response Methodology
Power premonition Response [PPR] refers to a methodology which includes a learning algorithm (not owned by PRIME, called PPRA) that enables proactive thermal control actions prior to high power (rate of change OR density) events,
while running test content during sort & class operations.
Previous thermal control techniques relied solely on reactive control methods, 
which resulted in large deviations from the desired test temperature. 
This deviation affects top bin splits when the junction temperature spikes above the desired 
test temperature & hurts the quality of test in terms of catching silicon defects when the 
junction temperature drops below the desired test temperature during the active area of the test. 

PPR for the purpose of this document refers to the entire system and its subcomponents that are involved
 in communicating the information that the algorithm will learn from, 
 as well as the subcomponents that will infer & implement the corrective actions at appropriate locations within the test program flow during run time.

The PPRA relies on the following pieces of input data to learn the proactive response over units.
 This data will be collected during run time on each unit in a lot, 
 by the PRIME service using TOS APIs & will be transmitted to the DPC engine via the existing 
 PUDL PPR Stream. 

1. Power consumed by the device during every test instance every 4ms 

2. Temperature readings (TDAU & DTS) on the DUT during every test instance at least every 4ms 

The PPRA will crunch the input data & attempt to converge on a set of thermal correcting actions.
The corrective actions are conveyed from the algorithm to PRIME via the Tool controller and the SITE-C APIs through a LUT (Look-Up Table), which will be applied during runtime for the next unit. From PRIME's point of view the LUT is an uservar 
that contains a JSON formatted string. **More information about the LUT is detailed in the PowerPremonitionResponseDeviceStartTestMethod wiki**.

All corrective actions are always taken on a per TI basis, and there are two categories:

**PreEmphasis method** 
(Pause Test instance execution -> Pre-emphasize (Cool-OR-heat) -> Resume test instance execution)  

This type of action will help with scenarios where the rate of change of power w.r.t time is sufficiently high. 
The thermal time constant is the primary reason for the temperature to not stay within the tolerance limits. 
In other words, the power changed significantly within a short period of time & caused a 
corresponding temperature spike & the thermal response was orders of magnitude slower & hence was ineffective. 
To accomplish the above a delta set point change will be introduced to cool/heat the DUT before the high-power test instance, 
followed by setting the delta set point change to zero & continuing in the regular power following mode for thermal control.
The delay for control setpoint operations gets handled by measuring the actual elapsed time since the last control set application. If the elapsed time is less than the required delay, it will only wait for the remaining time. This approach makes the process more efficient by avoiding unnecessary delays and only waiting for the exact amount of time needed.

 

**Power Density Method** 
(Alter the raw power that is sent over UEI to the TCC/DTC FW) 

This type of action will help with scenarios where the temperature offset from set point is relatively constant 
for relatively constant power. Essentially this phenomenon is not related to the mass of the thermal pedestal but is 
rather related to power density variations across the die, which cannot be adequately captured by the raw power signal alone. 
DTS readings (due to the increased spatiality) will certainly help with identifying the test instances that 
have a high-power density associated with them.  
In this mode the scaled power sent to the TCC/DTC FW, will increase/decrease the magnitude of cooling/heating & reduce 
the temperature offset for the entire duration of the test instance. In other words, the goal is to alter the cooling/heating response of the TCC/DTC FW to compensate for the local hot spots that are not previously visible in the power following or power density following mode.  
 
 Both types of corrective actions are implemented by PRIME based on the parameters coming from the LUT. 
 The details about the PRIME implementation will be explained in later sections.
# PRIME sequence for PPR 
-**INIT Flow**: The PowerPremonitionResponseInitTestMethod shall read the PPRCF (configuration file) file located in the PPR module of the TP. This file has the list of PPR TI. And create a shared storage object with the PPR Lot configuration.

-**Lot Start**: Starting on v11 PRIME is configured to open a custom PUDL stream called PPR and transmit it to the DPC.

-**Ituff Headers**: Starting on PRIME v11 the LotSession and DeviceSession (previously done by EVG) will print the Ituff Headers to the PPR PUDL stream if the uservar "SC_PPR_FUNCTIONAL_STATUS" is set to TRUE.

-**Test Plan Start**: PowerPremonitionResponseDeviceStartTestMethod shall read the **SC_PPR_LUT_CONTENT** uservar to get the LUT for the current DUT. And create a shared storage object with the PPR Device configuration.

-**MAIN Flow** (following actions are implemented by the combination of Pre/PostExecute() methods of the PowerPremonitionResponseService)

**PreExecute()**
  - Check if the next instance about to execute is a PPR tagged Test Instance (TI) from the PPRCF
  - If yes, look if there are PPR actions in the LUT for the PPR TI. 
  - Power Density actions:
    1. Apply the PowerScalingFactor from the LUT using the PowerSum HDMT resource
  - Follow the sequence below for PreEmphasis corrective actions:
    1. Cache the current “active” control set.
    2. Switch the control set (UeiClientPort0 HDMT resource) to the value designated by the PprControlSet field in the LUT
    3. Drive a Delta Set Point Change using the TemperatureOffset value listed in the LUT
    4. Monitor all the TDs on the die by sampling them every 4ms. 
    5. Wait until all the diodes are spotted at least once in a band defined by (ReleaseTemperature + ReleaseTemperatureUpperTolerance) & (ReleaseTemperature – ReleaseTemperatureLowerTolerance) OR if the time elapsed is => PreEmphasisTimeOut.
    6. As soon as the release criteria is met, switch the control set to the value cached at step 1, delayed by "PowerSurgeTime" LUT parameter.
  - Initialize the TOS sensor collection API buffers & start logging Power, TDAU and DTS profiles.

**PostExecute()**
  - Restore the PowerScalingFactor to the default value (1) using the PowerSum HDMT resource
  - Get data collection from TOS buffers that contain Power, TDAU and DTS profiles and log them to the PPR PUDL stream.

-**Ituff Footers**: The Prime LotSession and DeviceSession will print the Ituff footers to the PPR PUDL stream if the uservar "SC_PPR_FUNCTIONAL_STATUS" is set to TRUE.


*Notes*
1. For non-PPR test instance to be tested, if its preceding test instance is a PPR test instance, then do pre-emphasis (also called PostEmphasis or ExitPreEmphasis) with the release temperature set to a range of SC_TEMPERATURE - PprExitTempRangeUpper to SC_TEMPERATURE - PprExitTempRangeLower. 


#PPR PUDL STREAM
<p>This is a file similar to an Ituff that is sent to the DPC via PUDL to be processed by the PPRA.
The stream is initializated by TOS according to the customStream configuration included in each PRIME release (resources\customStream)</p>
